bytes.topic: Data Exploration — profiling datasets, understanding distributions, missing values
bytes.title: Jupyter Data Profiling — One Cell to Understand Any Dataset
bytes.body: Every exploration starts the same: shape, dtypes, nulls, distributions. In Jupyter, you can collapse this entire ritual into a single rich-output cell using ydata-profiling (formerly pandas-profiling). Install with `pip install ydata-profiling` and generate an interactive HTML report inline. The report renders directly in your notebook output cell — histograms, correlation matrices, missing-value heatmaps, duplicate row counts, and type warnings — all without writing plotting code. For large datasets, use `minimal=True` to skip expensive computations like correlations and text analysis. You can also call `profile.to_widgets()` instead of `to_notebook_iframe()` to get an ipywidgets-based interactive view that plays nicer with JupyterLab's rendering engine. Export reports via `profile.to_file("report.html")` to share with teammates who don't run notebooks. Combine this with `df.describe().T` in a preceding cell for a quick numeric sanity check before the full profile generates. This workflow turns a 20-minute manual EDA into a single Shift+Enter.
bytes.code_snippet:
import pandas as pd
from ydata_profiling import ProfileReport

df = pd.read_csv("dataset.csv")

# Quick sanity check
df.describe().T

# Full interactive profile in-cell
profile = ProfileReport(df, title="Dataset Profile", minimal=True)
profile.to_notebook_iframe()

# Export for sharing
profile.to_file("profile_report.html")

bytes.topic: Pandas Fundamentals — DataFrames, Series, indexing, groupby, merge/join
bytes.title: Jupyter + Pandas — Interactive DataFrame Exploration You Can't Do in Scripts
bytes.body:
Jupyter turns Pandas from a data library into a visual workbench. Every DataFrame expression in a cell auto-renders as an HTML table — no print() needed. Use df.head(), df.describe(), and df.info() in separate cells to iteratively profile data without rerunning your pipeline.

GroupBy results shine in notebooks: df.groupby('category')['revenue'].agg(['mean', 'sum']) renders instantly as a formatted table you can eyeball for anomalies. Chain .plot() on any aggregation and the chart appears inline.

Merge debugging is where Jupyter saves hours. Run the merge in one cell, then validate with df_merged.isna().sum() in the next to catch join mismatches immediately. Use pd.merge(left, right, on='key', how='left', indicator=True) and filter on _merge to see unmatched rows live.

Pro tip: Jupyter's last-expression display means you can end any cell with a Series or DataFrame slice — df.loc[df['score'] > 90, ['name', 'score']] — and get a rich table without wrapping it in display().
bytes.code_snippet:
import pandas as pd

# In Cell 1: Load and preview
df = pd.read_csv('sales.csv')
df.head(10)  # Jupyter renders as HTML table

# In Cell 2: GroupBy with inline plot
df.groupby('region')['revenue'].sum().plot(kind='bar', title='Revenue by Region')

# In Cell 3: Merge and validate join quality
orders = pd.read_csv('orders.csv')
merged = pd.merge(df, orders, on='customer_id', how='left', indicator=True)
merged['_merge'].value_counts()  # Spot unmatched rows instantly

bytes.topic: NumPy — array operations, broadcasting, vectorization vs loops, memory layout
bytes.title: Jupyter + NumPy — Visualize Broadcasting and Vectorization Speedups in Your Notebook
bytes.body:
Jupyter's `%%timeit` cell magic is the fastest way to prove why NumPy vectorization destroys Python loops. Broadcasting lets you operate on arrays of different shapes without copying data — but the performance difference is invisible until you measure it in a notebook cell.

Key insights for your Jupyter workflow:

**Memory layout matters.** NumPy defaults to C-contiguous (row-major) arrays. Column-wise operations on C-order arrays thrash cache. Use `np.asfortranarray()` when your workload is column-heavy, then verify with `arr.flags` right in a cell.

**Broadcasting rules:** dimensions align from the right. Shape `(3,1)` broadcasts with `(1,4)` to produce `(3,4)` — no explicit `np.tile()` needed.

**Always benchmark in-place.** Jupyter's `%timeit` runs multiple loops automatically, giving stable measurements. Compare vectorized ops against `for` loops on the same array to build intuition about 10–100× speedups. Use `%%timeit -n 1000` for fine-grained control over iteration count.
bytes.code_snippet:
import numpy as np

# --- Cell 1: Broadcasting demo ---
a = np.arange(3).reshape(3, 1)  # shape (3,1)
b = np.arange(4).reshape(1, 4)  # shape (1,4)
result = a * b  # broadcasts to (3,4)
print(f"Shapes: {a.shape} * {b.shape} -> {result.shape}")
print(result)

# --- Cell 2: Vectorization vs loop (use %%timeit in real notebook) ---
arr = np.random.randn(1_000_000)
%timeit arr ** 2 + 2 * arr + 1          # vectorized: ~2ms
%timeit np.array([x**2 + 2*x + 1 for x in arr])  # loop: ~800ms

# --- Cell 3: Check memory layout ---
print(arr.flags['C_CONTIGUOUS'])  # True by default

bytes.topic: Polars — lazy evaluation, expressions API, performance vs Pandas, when to switch
bytes.title: Jupyter + Polars — Lazy Evaluation That Makes Your Notebook Cells Fly
bytes.body:
Polars' lazy API is a perfect fit for Jupyter's cell-by-cell workflow. Instead of eagerly computing every transformation like Pandas, you build a query plan across cells and call `.collect()` only when you need results. This means you can chain filters, joins, and aggregations in separate cells without triggering execution until you're ready.

The expressions API replaces Pandas' bracket indexing with composable, readable operations. `pl.col('price').filter(pl.col('category') == 'GPU').mean()` reads like intent, not implementation. In Jupyter, this matters — notebooks are documentation. Readable chains in cells tell a story.

Performance-wise, Polars runs multi-threaded by default. A GroupBy on 10M rows that takes 4s in Pandas finishes in ~400ms. The lazy engine also optimizes predicate pushdown and projection pruning automatically — optimizations you'd have to manually arrange in Pandas.

Switch to Polars when: your DataFrames exceed 1M rows, you're chaining 3+ transformations, or your notebook cells are blocking on computation. Keep Pandas for small EDA or libraries that require it.
bytes.code_snippet:
import polars as pl

# In Cell 1: build lazy query plan (no computation yet)
q = (
    pl.scan_csv("benchmarks.csv")  # lazy reader
    .filter(pl.col("framework") == "pytorch")
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("throughput") / pl.col("batch_size")).alias("per_sample_ms")
    )
    .group_by("gpu_model")
    .agg(pl.col("per_sample_ms").mean())
)

# In Cell 2: execute and display — Jupyter renders the DataFrame
q.collect()

bytes.topic: Data Cleaning — handling nulls, outlier detection, type coercion, deduplication
bytes.title: Jupyter Notebooks — Interactive Data Cleaning Pipeline with Cell-by-Cell Validation
bytes.body: Jupyter's cell-based execution model is ideal for data cleaning because you can inspect results at every stage. The pattern: one cleaning operation per cell, always display shape and sample output below. Start by profiling nulls with `df.isnull().sum()` in its own cell — Jupyter renders the Series as a formatted table automatically. For outlier detection, use a cell to compute IQR bounds and a follow-up cell with a histogram via `df[col].hist()` — inline plots (`%matplotlib inline`) let you visually confirm before filtering. Type coercion is where Jupyter shines: run `df.dtypes` in one cell, apply `pd.to_numeric(df[col], errors='coerce')` in the next, then re-check dtypes immediately. Coerced values become NaN, visible in the same notebook flow. For deduplication, display `df.duplicated().sum()` before and after `df.drop_duplicates()` in adjacent cells. Use `%%time` cell magic on expensive operations to catch slow cleaning steps. This inspect-transform-verify loop per cell is what makes Jupyter the natural environment for data cleaning — every intermediate state is visible and reproducible.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Cell 1: Profile nulls
print(f"Shape: {df.shape}")
df.isnull().sum().sort_values(ascending=False)

# Cell 2: Detect outliers via IQR
%matplotlib inline
Q1, Q3 = df['value'].quantile([0.25, 0.75])
IQR = Q3 - Q1
mask = df['value'].between(Q1 - 1.5*IQR, Q3 + 1.5*IQR)
print(f"Outliers removed: {(~mask).sum()}")
df = df[mask]

# Cell 3: Coerce types and deduplicate
df['price'] = pd.to_numeric(df['price'], errors='coerce')
df = df.drop_duplicates()
%%time
df.info()

bytes.topic: Feature Engineering — encoding categoricals, scaling, binning, interaction features
bytes.title: Jupyter Feature Engineering — Interactive Encoding, Scaling & Binning in One Notebook
bytes.body:
Jupyter's cell-by-cell execution makes feature engineering iterative and visual. Build your pipeline incrementally: encode categoricals in one cell, inspect value distributions with .value_counts() inline, then scale numerics in the next. The key advantage is immediate feedback — call df.head() after each transform to catch issues before they propagate.

Use pandas + scikit-learn together. OrdinalEncoder for ordinal cats, OneHotEncoder (via pd.get_dummies) for nominals. For scaling, StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler fit in one cell, transform in the next — keeping fitted objects in memory across cells. Binning with pd.cut() or pd.qcut() lets you visualize bin distributions instantly with df['binned'].hist() rendered inline.

Interaction features multiply or combine columns — create them, then use Jupyter's built-in display to render correlation heatmaps (sns.heatmap(df.corr())) without leaving the notebook. This tight feedback loop between transform and visualization is what makes Jupyter the default environment for feature engineering workflows.
bytes.code_snippet:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# Encode categoricals inline — inspect immediately
df = pd.get_dummies(df, columns=['category'], drop_first=True)
df.head()  # Jupyter renders this as a rich HTML table

# Scale numerics, keep scaler in memory for later cells
scaler = StandardScaler()
df[['price', 'quantity']] = scaler.fit_transform(df[['price', 'quantity']])

# Bin continuous feature and visualize distribution inline
df['price_bin'] = pd.qcut(df['price'], q=4, labels=['low','mid','high','top'])
df['price_bin'].value_counts().plot.bar()  # renders inline with %matplotlib inline

# Interaction feature + instant correlation check
df['price_x_qty'] = df['price'] * df['quantity']

bytes.topic: Exploratory Data Analysis — correlation analysis, pivot tables, aggregation patterns
bytes.title: Jupyter EDA Toolkit — Correlation Heatmaps, Pivot Tables & GroupBy in One Notebook
bytes.body:
Jupyter's cell-based execution makes iterative EDA fast — run a correlation matrix, tweak the visualization, re-run just that cell. The key pattern: keep data loading in cell 1, transformations in cell 2, and visualizations in cell 3+. This lets you modify aggregation logic without reloading data.

For correlation analysis, `df.corr()` paired with Seaborn's `heatmap()` renders inline thanks to `%matplotlib inline`. Use `display()` instead of `print()` for DataFrames — Jupyter's rich display renders styled HTML tables with automatic truncation.

Pivot tables via `pd.pivot_table()` with `aggfunc=['mean','count']` give you multi-metric summaries. Chain `.style.background_gradient()` to get conditional formatting directly in the notebook output — no export needed.

For grouped aggregations, `df.groupby().agg()` with named aggregations keeps output readable. Pro tip: use `%%time` cell magic to benchmark heavy groupby operations, and `df.memory_usage(deep=True)` in a separate cell to watch memory before scaling up.
bytes.code_snippet:
%matplotlib inline
import pandas as pd, seaborn as sns, matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = pd.read_csv("data.csv")

# Cell 2: Correlation heatmap
corr = df.select_dtypes("number").corr()
sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, fmt=".2f", cmap="coolwarm", center=0)
plt.title("Feature Correlations")
plt.tight_layout()

# Cell 3: Pivot table with styled output
pivot = pd.pivot_table(df, values="revenue", index="region",
                       columns="quarter", aggfunc=["mean", "count"])
display(pivot.style.background_gradient(cmap="YlGn"))

bytes.topic: Matplotlib — figure/axes API, customization, subplots, publication-quality charts
bytes.title: Jupyter Matplotlib — Publication-Quality Charts with the Figure/Axes API
bytes.body:
The pyplot shortcut (`plt.plot()`) breaks down fast in Jupyter notebooks when you need multi-panel figures or fine-grained control. Switch to the explicit Figure/Axes API: `fig, axes = plt.subplots()` gives you direct handles to every element.

In Jupyter, pair this with `%matplotlib inline` or `%matplotlib widget` (for interactive zoom/pan via ipympl). The widget backend is especially powerful — resize and inspect figures live without re-running cells.

For publication quality, control the figure at creation: set `figsize`, `dpi`, and `constrained_layout=True` to avoid overlapping labels. Style individual axes via `ax.set_xlabel()`, `ax.tick_params()`, and `ax.spines` — never rely on global `plt.rcParams` mid-notebook, as it silently mutates state across cells.

Use `fig.savefig('plot.pdf', bbox_inches='tight')` from the same cell as your plot — Jupyter's inline backend closes figures after cell execution, so saving in a later cell produces blank output. Subplots share axes with `sharey=True` to declutter repeated labels.
bytes.code_snippet:
%matplotlib inline
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

x = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 200)
fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4),
                                sharey=True, constrained_layout=True, dpi=150)
ax1.plot(x, np.sin(x), color='#2563eb', linewidth=2)
ax1.set_title('Sine'); ax1.set_xlabel('Radians'); ax1.set_ylabel('Amplitude')
ax2.plot(x, np.cos(x), color='#dc2626', linewidth=2)
ax2.set_title('Cosine'); ax2.set_xlabel('Radians')
for ax in (ax1, ax2):
    ax.spines[['top', 'right']].set_visible(False)
fig.savefig('trig_plots.pdf', bbox_inches='tight')

bytes.topic: Seaborn — statistical visualizations, themes, integration with Pandas
bytes.title: Jupyter + Seaborn — Inline Statistical Plots That Just Work with Pandas
bytes.body:
Seaborn is built on matplotlib but designed for Pandas DataFrames, making it the fastest path from raw data to publication-quality statistical plots inside Jupyter. The magic starts with `%matplotlib inline` — every Seaborn call renders directly below the cell, no `plt.show()` needed.

Seaborn's `set_theme()` instantly upgrades your entire notebook's visual style. Call it once in your first cell and every subsequent plot inherits clean aesthetics. For EDA, `sns.pairplot(df)` generates a full correlation matrix of scatterplots in one line — something that would take dozens of matplotlib calls.

The real power is statistical integration. Functions like `sns.lmplot()` and `sns.boxenplot()` compute regressions and distributions on the fly. Pass a DataFrame column name as `hue` to split by category automatically. Seaborn reads your DataFrame's dtypes and adjusts axis formatting.

Pro tip: use `sns.set_context('notebook')` specifically — it optimizes font sizes and line weights for Jupyter's rendered cell output width, not slides or papers.
bytes.code_snippet:
# First cell — run once
%matplotlib inline
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd

sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid")
sns.set_context("notebook")

# Load and plot in one cell
df = sns.load_dataset("penguins").dropna()
sns.pairplot(df, hue="species", height=2.5)

# Next cell — regression with confidence interval
sns.lmplot(data=df, x="flipper_length_mm", y="body_mass_g",
           hue="species", height=5, aspect=1.4)

bytes.topic: Jupyter — notebook best practices, reproducibility, parameterized notebooks
bytes.title: Jupyter Notebooks — Reproducibility With Papermill and Cell Discipline
bytes.body:
Most Jupyter notebooks rot within weeks because they rely on hidden state from out-of-order cell execution. Fix this with two practices.

First, enforce top-to-bottom reproducibility. Restart Kernel and Run All should be your merge gate. Use `nbconvert --execute` in CI to catch notebooks that fail on a clean run. If a cell depends on a variable defined 30 cells up, that's a smell — extract it into a function or a shared utils module imported at the top.

Second, parameterize with Papermill. Tag a cell as `parameters` in cell metadata, then execute the notebook programmatically with injected values. This turns notebooks into reusable pipelines — run the same analysis across datasets, date ranges, or model configs without copy-pasting .ipynb files.

Keep cells short (one logical step each), pin dependencies in the first cell with `%pip install package==version`, and never rely on IPython's In/Out history for downstream logic. Treat notebooks as scripts that happen to have rich output, not scratch pads.
bytes.code_snippet:
import papermill as pm

# Tag a cell in your notebook with 'parameters' metadata,
# then execute with injected values:
pm.execute_notebook(
    input_path="analysis_template.ipynb",
    output_path="output/analysis_q1_2025.ipynb",
    parameters={
        "dataset_path": "data/q1_2025.parquet",
        "start_date": "2025-01-01",
        "target_column": "churn",
    },
    kernel_name="python3",
)

bytes.topic: Performance — vectorization, chunking large files, memory-efficient operations
bytes.title: Jupyter %%timeit and Chunked DataFrames — Process Million-Row CSVs Without Crashing Your Kernel
bytes.body:
Jupyter kernels share a single memory pool across all cells. Loading a 2GB CSV with pd.read_csv() in one shot often triggers a MemoryError or kills the kernel silently. The fix: use the chunksize parameter to stream data in batches, processing each chunk with vectorized NumPy/Pandas ops instead of Python loops.

Wrap your pipeline in %%timeit to benchmark cell execution directly in-notebook. Combine this with %memit (from memory_profiler) to track per-cell memory deltas — critical when your kernel's RAM is capped by your machine or a JupyterHub resource limit.

Key patterns: replace iterrows() with .apply() or direct column arithmetic (true vectorization). Use df.astype('category') for repeated string columns — this alone can cut memory 80%. For exploratory work, load only needed columns via usecols. Always call del df and gc.collect() in cleanup cells to release memory before the next pipeline stage. Monitor with !jupyter kernelspec list to verify you're on the right env.
bytes.code_snippet:
%load_ext memory_profiler

import pandas as pd
import gc

# Stream large CSV in 50k-row chunks
results = []
for chunk in pd.read_csv('events.csv', chunksize=50_000, usecols=['ts', 'value', 'category']):
    chunk['category'] = chunk['category'].astype('category')
    chunk['value_norm'] = (chunk['value'] - chunk['value'].mean()) / chunk['value'].std()
    results.append(chunk.groupby('category')['value_norm'].mean())

summary = pd.concat(results).groupby(level=0).mean()
del results; gc.collect()  # free memory for next cell
summary

bytes.topic: Time Series — resampling, rolling windows, lag features, seasonality
bytes.title: Jupyter Time Series Toolkit — Resampling, Rolling Windows & Lag Features in One Notebook
bytes.body: Jupyter's cell-by-cell execution makes it ideal for iterative time series exploration. Start by resampling raw timestamps to uniform intervals using pandas' `.resample()` — visualize the before/after inline with `%matplotlib inline` to catch gaps immediately. Next, apply `.rolling()` windows to smooth noise; Jupyter lets you tweak window sizes and re-execute the cell to visually compare 7-day vs 30-day moving averages without rerunning your entire pipeline. For lag features, use `.shift()` to create autoregressive inputs — display the correlation matrix with `df.corr()` right below to validate signal strength interactively. Seasonality detection benefits from Jupyter's rich output: plot `seasonal_decompose()` results from statsmodels, which renders the trend, seasonal, and residual components as a multi-panel figure directly in the notebook. Pro tip: use `%%time` cell magic to benchmark your feature engineering steps, and `display(df.head())` between transforms to catch shape mismatches early. This inspect-as-you-go workflow is what makes Jupyter unbeatable for time series EDA.
bytes.code_snippet:
import pandas as pd
from statsmodels.tsa.seasonal import seasonal_decompose
%matplotlib inline

df = pd.read_csv('sensor_data.csv', parse_dates=['timestamp'], index_col='timestamp')
df_hourly = df['value'].resample('1h').mean().interpolate()

df_hourly.rolling(window=24).mean().plot(title='24h Rolling Mean')

df_feat = df_hourly.to_frame()
df_feat['lag_1'] = df_feat['value'].shift(1)
df_feat['lag_24'] = df_feat['value'].shift(24)

result = seasonal_decompose(df_hourly, period=24)
result.plot();

bytes.topic: Statistical Testing — hypothesis tests, p-values, confidence intervals, when they matter
bytes.title: Jupyter Notebooks for Statistical Testing — Interactive Hypothesis Testing Done Right
bytes.body: Jupyter's cell-based execution model is ideal for statistical testing workflows: state a hypothesis in a markdown cell, run the test in code, then interpret results inline — all in one reproducible document. Use `scipy.stats` for hypothesis tests and render results directly beneath your analysis. A common pitfall: running multiple t-tests across cells without correction inflates false positives. Use `statsmodels.stats.multitest` for Bonferroni or FDR correction. For confidence intervals, `scipy.stats.t.interval()` gives you exact intervals you can display with `IPython.display.Markdown` for formatted output. Pro tip: leverage Jupyter's `%store` magic to persist test results across notebooks when comparing experiments. Structure your notebook as: (1) markdown cell with H₀/H₁, (2) data loading cell, (3) assumption checks (normality via `shapiro`), (4) test execution, (5) markdown interpretation. This pattern makes your statistical reasoning auditable and reproducible — critical when sharing analysis with teams.
bytes.code_snippet:
from scipy import stats
import numpy as np
from IPython.display import Markdown, display

np.random.seed(42)
group_a = np.random.normal(loc=50, scale=10, size=100)
group_b = np.random.normal(loc=53, scale=10, size=100)

# Assumption check
_, p_norm = stats.shapiro(group_a)
display(Markdown(f"**Normality check (Shapiro):** p={p_norm:.4f}"))

# Two-sample t-test
t_stat, p_val = stats.ttest_ind(group_a, group_b)
ci = stats.t.interval(0.95, df=len(group_a)-1, loc=np.mean(group_a), scale=stats.sem(group_a))

display(Markdown(f"**t={t_stat:.3f}, p={p_val:.4f}**\n\n95% CI for Group A mean: ({ci[0]:.2f}, {ci[1]:.2f})"))

bytes.topic: Data Validation — schema validation with Pandera or Great Expectations, catching bad data early
bytes.title: Jupyter + Pandera — Validate DataFrames Before They Wreck Your Analysis
bytes.body:
Every Jupyter notebook that loads external data should validate it before the first `.describe()`. Pandera lets you define DataFrame schemas as code and run them inline, turning silent data bugs into loud, traceable errors right in your cell output.

Define a schema once, then call `schema.validate(df)` in the cell immediately after your `pd.read_csv()`. When validation fails, Pandera raises a `SchemaError` with a detailed failure case DataFrame — rendered natively in Jupyter's rich output. This beats scrolling through `.info()` and `.isnull().sum()` manually.

Key Jupyter workflow trick: put your schema in an early cell and use `%run` or `%load` to share it across notebooks in the same project. This prevents schema drift when multiple notebooks consume the same dataset.

For heavier pipelines, chain `schema.validate(df)` with `assert_frame_equal` in a dedicated validation cell that acts as a gate — downstream cells won't execute if you stop on error. Pandera also supports hypothesis testing via `pa.Hypothesis`, letting you embed statistical checks directly in your EDA flow.
bytes.code_snippet:
import pandera as pa
from pandera import Column, Check
import pandas as pd

schema = pa.DataFrameSchema({
    "age": Column(int, Check.in_range(0, 120)),
    "email": Column(str, Check.str_matches(r".+@.+\..+")),
    "score": Column(float, Check.in_range(0.0, 1.0)),
    "label": Column(str, Check.isin(["positive", "negative", "neutral"])),
})

df = pd.read_csv("dataset.csv")
validated_df = schema.validate(df)  # raises SchemaError with rich output in Jupyter
validated_df.head()

bytes.topic: Working with Large Data — chunking, Dask, sampling strategies, when to graduate to Spark
bytes.title: Jupyter Notebooks and Large Data — Chunking, Dask, and Knowing When to Leave
bytes.body:
Jupyter's interactive model breaks down when your DataFrame exceeds available RAM. Before reaching for a cluster, try these in-notebook strategies.

**Chunked reading** lets you process CSVs without loading everything at once. Use `pd.read_csv(chunksize=...)` and aggregate incrementally inside a loop — each chunk is a regular DataFrame you can inspect cell-by-cell.

**Dask** is the natural next step. It mirrors the pandas API but executes lazily, partitioning your data across cores. The Dask JupyterLab extension adds a dashboard widget showing task graphs and worker memory directly in your notebook — invaluable for spotting skewed partitions.

**Sampling** is underrated. Use `df.sample(frac=0.01, random_state=42)` for EDA, then run full computation only when your logic is validated. Jupyter's cell-based workflow makes this toggle trivial.

**Graduate to Spark** when your data exceeds a single machine's disk, needs fault-tolerant shuffles, or when Dask task graphs become unmanageably deep. PySpark's `findspark` integration works in Jupyter, but at that point consider migrating to scheduled scripts.
bytes.code_snippet:
import dask.dataframe as dd

# Read a large CSV lazily — Jupyter won't spike in memory
ddf = dd.read_csv("events_*.csv", blocksize="64MB")

# Familiar pandas-like API, but lazy
result = (
    ddf.groupby("user_id")["event_type"]
    .count()
    .nlargest(10)
    .compute()  # triggers execution; inspect in next cell
)

# Quick EDA on a sample first
sample = ddf.sample(frac=0.01).compute()
sample.head()

bytes.topic: Reproducibility — random seeds, environment pinning, notebook-to-script conversion
bytes.title: Jupyter Reproducibility — Seeds, Pinning, and Escaping Notebook Chaos
bytes.body:
Jupyter notebooks are reproducibility nightmares by default. Out-of-order cell execution, hidden state, and unpinned dependencies make results impossible to replicate. Fix this systematically.

First, pin your entire environment. Use %pip install inside notebooks instead of !pip — it guarantees installation into the kernel's environment, not a random system Python. Export with !pip freeze > requirements.txt in a dedicated cell.

Second, seed everything in Cell 1. NumPy, random, and framework-specific RNGs all need explicit seeds. Use a single SEED constant and propagate it.

Third, eliminate hidden state. Restart Kernel & Run All before every commit. If it fails, your notebook has order-dependent bugs. The %reset magic clears the namespace for targeted debugging.

Finally, convert to scripts for production. jupyter nbconvert --to script strips markdown and cell metadata, producing a linear .py file. Pair this with papermill for parameterized, headless execution — it runs notebooks as functions with injected parameters and captures output cells as artifacts.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Cell 1 — Reproducibility preamble
import random, numpy as np

SEED = 42
random.seed(SEED)
np.random.seed(SEED)

# Pin deps to kernel's env (not !pip)
%pip install pandas==2.2.2 scikit-learn==1.5.1 --quiet

# Convert notebook to script from terminal:
# jupyter nbconvert --to script analysis.ipynb

# Run headless with parameters:
# papermill analysis.ipynb output.ipynb -p SEED 123

bytes.topic: Visualization Best Practices — choosing the right chart, color accessibility, avoiding chartjunk
bytes.title: Jupyter Notebook Visualization — Choosing Charts, Accessible Colors, and Killing Chartjunk
bytes.body:
Jupyter's inline rendering (`%matplotlib inline` or `%matplotlib widget`) makes it easy to over-plot. Fight that instinct.

**Pick the right chart:** Use `sns.histplot` for distributions, `sns.scatterplot` for correlations, `sns.barplot` for categorical comparisons. Avoid pie charts — horizontal bar charts are always more readable in notebook cells.

**Color accessibility:** Default matplotlib palettes fail WCAG contrast checks. Switch to `cmap='cividis'` or Seaborn's `colorblind` palette — both are perceptually uniform and safe for deuteranopia. Set it globally in your first cell so every plot inherits it.

**Strip the chartjunk:** Matplotlib's defaults add redundant spines, gridlines, and ticks. Use `sns.despine()` after every plot and set `plt.tight_layout()` to prevent label clipping in notebook output cells. For interactive exploration, `%matplotlib widget` with `ipywidgets` sliders beats generating 20 static plots.

**Pro tip:** Call `plt.rcParams.update()` in a setup cell to enforce consistent figure sizes (`figsize`), DPI, and font scales across your entire notebook.
bytes.code_snippet:
# First cell — global notebook viz defaults
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

%matplotlib inline
sns.set_palette("colorblind")
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (8, 4),
    "figure.dpi": 120,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "font.size": 11,
})

bytes.topic: Data Versioning — tracking dataset versions alongside model versions
bytes.title: Jupyter Notebooks — Version Your Datasets With Cell-Level DVC Tracking
bytes.body:
Every notebook checkpoint saves code state, but the DataFrame you trained on vanishes the moment you overwrite the variable. DVC (Data Version Control) integrates directly into Jupyter cells to hash and track dataset snapshots alongside your .ipynb file.

Install with `%pip install dvc`. Then use `%%capture` and shell magics to version data without leaving the notebook. Each `dvc add` hashes your dataset file and creates a `.dvc` pointer file that Git tracks instead of the raw data. When you `dvc checkout` a prior commit, the exact dataset is restored.

The key insight: pair `%store` or `%%writefile` with DVC to snapshot intermediate DataFrames. Export a filtered DataFrame to Parquet mid-notebook, `!dvc add` it, and commit. Now `git log --oneline` shows model code changes and `dvc diff` shows which dataset version each experiment used. Tag notebook checkpoints with `!git tag exp-v3` so you can reproduce any cell output by restoring both code and data state together. No more 'which CSV did that F1 score come from?'
bytes.code_snippet:
import pandas as pd

# After your data cleaning cells:
df_clean.to_parquet("data/train_v2.parquet")

# Version the dataset from inside the notebook
!dvc add data/train_v2.parquet
!git add data/train_v2.parquet.dvc data/.gitignore
!git commit -m "dataset: v2 after outlier removal"
!git tag exp-v2-clean

# Later, restore this exact dataset in any notebook:
# !git checkout exp-v2-clean
# !dvc checkout
df_restored = pd.read_parquet("data/train_v2.parquet")
